perturbation.original_text: Pursuant to the doctrine of sequential liability, payment from Kubient to Customer shall be made within seven (7) days from Kubient's receipt of payment, regardless of when Customer submits an invoice.
perturbation.changed_text: Pursuant to the doctrine of sequential liability, payment from Kubient to Customer shall be made within thirty (60) days from Kubient's receipt of payment, regardless of when Customer submits an invoice.
perturbation.explanation: Extended the payment timeframe to contradict potential prompt payment laws. Some states require faster payment terms, especially for certain industries.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York State Prompt Payment Law
perturbation.law_explanation: New York's prompt payment law for certain construction contracts requires payment within specific timeframes, often shorter than 60 days, unless otherwise agreed upon in writing and deemed reasonable. Delaying payment to 60 days may violate this if the conditions are met.
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the payment timeframe from 7 days to 60 days. The Law Explanation states that New York's prompt payment law for construction contracts requires payment within specific timeframes, often shorter than 60 days, unless otherwise agreed upon in writing and deemed reasonable. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions Article 35-E of the General Business law concerning construction contracts in New York. Thus, extending the payment timeframe to 60 days directly contradicts potential prompt payment laws in New York, making the contractual terms potentially unenforceable or illegal under Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: No action, suit or proceeding shall be brought against a Party more than one year after the date of termination this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: No action, suit or proceeding shall be brought against a Party more than six months after the date of termination this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Reduced the statute of limitations for claims, which may conflict with standard contract law and applicable state laws regarding breach of contract.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Statute of Limitations for Breach of Contract
perturbation.law_explanation: New York's statute of limitations for breach of contract is six years. Reducing the contractual limit to six months is significantly shorter and potentially unenforceable.

Civil Practice Law & Rules (CVP) CHAPTER 8, ARTICLE 2 § 213. Actions to be commenced within six years: where not otherwise provided for; on contract; on sealed instrument; on bond or note, and mortgage upon real property; by state based on misappropriation of public property;

Laws (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 New York Laws CVP - Civil Practice Law and Rules Article 2 - Limitations of Time 213 - Actions to Be Commenced Within Six Years: Where Not Otherwise Provided For; on Contract; on Sealed Instrument; on Bond or Note, and Mortgage Upon Real Universal Citation: NY CPLR § 213 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next § 213. Actions to be commenced within six years: where not otherwise provided for; on contract; on sealed instrument; on bond or note, and mortgage upon real property; by state based on misappropriation of public property; based on mistake; by corporation against director, officer or stockholder; based on fraud. The following actions must be commenced within six years: 1. an action for which no limitation is specifically prescribed by law; 2. an action upon a contractual obligation or liability, express or implied, except as provided in section two hundred thirteen-a or two hundred fourteen-i of this article or article 2 of the uniform commercial code or article 36-B of the general business law; 3. an action upon a sealed instrument; 4. an action upon a bond or note, the payment of which is secured by a mortgage upon real property, or upon a bond or note and mortgage so secured, or upon a mortgage of real property, or any interest therein; (a) In any action on an instrument described under this subdivision, if the statute of limitations is raised as a defense, and if that defense is based on a claim that the instrument at issue was accelerated
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the statute of limitations for bringing a legal action against a party to six months, while New York law provides a six-year statute of limitations for breach of contract. This directly contradicts the state law, as evidenced by Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2, both citing NY CPLR § 213, which states actions upon a contractual obligation must be commenced within six years. The modification weakens the legal protection afforded by the state's statute of limitations, making the contractual term unenforceable and creating a legal inconsistency.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient will protect the Confidential Information, for one (1) year from the date of disclosure, by using at least the same degree of care as it uses to protect its own Confidential Information, but no less than a reasonable degree of care, to prevent unauthorized use, disclosure or publication.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient will protect the Confidential Information, for six (6) months from the date of disclosure, by using at least the same degree of care as it uses to protect its own Confidential Information, but no less than a reasonable degree of care, to prevent unauthorized use, disclosure or publication.
perturbation.explanation: Shortened the confidentiality protection period, which could be unreasonably short depending on the nature of the information, potentially conflicting with trade secret laws if the information qualifies as a trade secret.
perturbation.law_explanation: While New York's trade secret law does not specify a fixed duration for protection, a six-month protection period may be deemed insufficient to protect legitimate trade secrets, depending on the industry and the nature of the confidential information, potentially undermining legal protection afforded to trade secrets. The reasonableness of the protection is based on the confidential information's useful life. Six months may be deemed inappropriate.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: NYS Open Legislation | NYSenate.gov Sorry, you need to enable JavaScript to visit this website. Skip to main content The requested entry could not be found. Try searching OpenLegislation Browse all Laws of New York
perturbation.justification: The modification reduces the confidentiality protection period from one year to six months. The Law Explanation suggests that this shorter period may be insufficient to protect legitimate trade secrets, potentially undermining legal protection if the confidential information has a longer useful life, thus creating a contradiction. The Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction definition states that a contradiction arises when time-sensitive obligations do not align with legal requirements, and this change sets a deadline that may violate federal or state law concerning trade secret protection.
